title: Tachyarrhythmias
content_markdown:
#### Narrow QRS Complex
**Consider pharmacological treatment if heart rate is:**

- Adult: > 150/min in adults.
- Paediatric: > 180/min (if symptomatic / associated with poor perfusion). 

- 12 lead ECG.
- Modified Valsalva 
- Repeat once if unsuccessful.
- **ICP: If SVT:**
- Adenosine.
- If **rapid AF** or **A.Flutter:**
- Refer to **Rapid Atrial Fibrillation / Flutter** section in this CMG.
- Consider **IV fluids** in all tachycardic patients if: 
- Hypotensive, **and**
- Not in LVF, **and**- Unresponsive to pharmacological management.
- Any rapid rhythm (> 200/min) in an unconscious patient with no pulse: 
- **Defibrillate.**

#### Wide QRS Complex
**Consider treatment if heart rate is:**

- Adult: > 150/min.
- Paediatric: > 180/min (if symptomatic / associated with poor perfusion).

- Make differentiation (12 lead ECG if time).
- If aberrant SVT, AF or A.Flutter: 
- Refer to **Rapid Atrial Fibrillation / Flutter** section in this CMG.
- No pulse: 
- Treat as **shockable** cardiac arrest
- CMG 4 Cardiac Arrest – Adult - CMG 5 Cardiac Arrest – Paediatric.
- Any rapid rhythm (>200/min) in an unconscious patient with no pulse: 
- **Defibrillate.**
- **ICP: Symptomatic VT** with cardiac output: 
- Amiodarone
- **If amiodarone contraindicated:** lignocaine. - **ICP: Torsades De Pointes: **
- Magnesium sulphate as first medication
- Do not use amiodarone.
- Consider **IV fluids** in all tachycardic patients if: 
- Hypotensive, and
- Not in LVF (especially if unresponsive to management).
#### Rapid Atrial Fibrillation or Flutter
**Establish diagnostic criteria:**

- Atrial fibrillation or flutter, rapid rate 
- Adult: > 150/min
- Paediatric: > 180/min.

- Recent onset of AF (reliably CMG 14 Shock and Hypoperfusion.

- **ICP:** If LVF and hypotension: 
- Treat **cautiously** with amiodarone.
#### Ischaemic Chest Pain
- Treat chest pain as appropriate.
- **ICP:** Treat rapid rate with amiodarone concurrently.#### Pulmonary Oedema
- Treat pulmonary oedema as appropriate.
- **ICP:** Treat rapid rate with amiodarone concurrently.
- **Monitor BP closely**
- The combination of drug treatments for pulmonary oedema may cause hypotension.
#### Secondary to an Acute Cerebral Event
These patients will present with a \u2193 LOC and may be hypotensive.

- If no LVF: 
- Treat with IV fluids as per CMG 14 Shock and Hypoperfusion.

- **Do not** use amiodarone in these patients.
**Note:** unconscious patients post cardiac arrest in rapid AF may be treated with amiodarone unless otherwise contraindicated. Allow several minutes for rhythms to stabilise **before** administering amiodarone.

Amiodarone.text: Formula: 5mg/kg Preparation: 150mg in 3mls Dose: 300mg Volume: 6ml Volume: 6ml Formula: 50mg/kg Preparation: 2.5g in 5mls Dose: 2.5g Volume: 5ml

##### Type
Potent anti-arrhythmic agent [S4]

##### Presentations
150mg in 3ml – glass ampoule

##### Actions
Complex electrophysiological and pharmacological profile:

- Prolongs the action potential duration; increases the refractoriness of all cardiac tissues

- Blocks Na+ channels (class I action)

- Has some anti-adrenergic effects (class II action)

- Ca2+ blockade (class IV action)

- Prolongs QT interval – reflects global prolongation of repolarisation 

- When given IV, there is a significant effect on the AV node which causes a delay in nodal conduction

- Also effective for accessory pathway conduction 

#### Uses
Effective for both supraventricular and ventricular arrhythmias. ACTAS uses: 

- To slow the ventricular rate in atrial fibrillation and flutter 

- To treat VF and VT

#### Adverse Effects
When given IV, can cause vasodilation, negative inotropic effects and hypotension (dose and rate dependent).

Occasionally may cause:

- Bradycardia (especially in older patients)

- Phlebitis

- Hot flushes / sweating

#### Contraindications

- Known hypersensitivity

- Cross sensitivity to iodine

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Only use in the setting of cardiac arrest or peri-arrest

##### Breastfeeding
- Approved for administration in ACTAS setting, document breastfeeding status in ePCR

#### Doses#### VF/VT cardiac arrest##### Adult
- **ICP:** 300mg IV/IO 
- Over 30-60 seconds.

##### Paediatric
- **ICP:** 5mg/kg IV/IO (to maximum 300mg) 
- Over 30-60 seconds.

#### VT, AF and A.flutter - with cardiac output##### Adult
- **ICP:** 150mg IV via Springfusor 
- Made up to 7ml total volume with normal saline (7ml will run over 10 minutes)
- VT – if extremely compromised, may be given over 5 minutes by slow IV injection
- No repeat dose for either treatment regime.
##### Paediatric
- **ICP:** 5mg/kg IV (to maximum 150mg) via Springfusor 
- Made up to 7ml total volume with normal saline (7ml will run over 10 minutes)
- VT – if extremely compromised, may be given over 5 minutes by slow IV injection
- No repeat dose for either treatment regime.
#### Special Notes
- Significant potential drug interactions.
- The following may potentiate the actions of amiodarone: 
- Digoxin
- Phenytoin (Dilantin)
- \u03b2 blockers- Ca2+ channel blocker- Other antiarrhythmics.

Magnesium.text: Formula: 50mg/kg Preparation: 2.5g in 5mls Dose: 350mg Volume: 1.4ml (when diluted to 10ml)
Postpartum haemorrhage is generally defined as an acute haemorrhage with blood loss of ≥ 500ml occurring within 12 weeks post‐delivery. Severe PPH is defined as ≥ 1,000ml of blood loss.

Magnesium is the second most abundant intracellular cation. Less than 1% is present in extracellular fluid. Magnesium is involved in the processes regulating sodium and potassium movement across cell membranes and, as such, it may promote myocardial cell membrane stability.
2.5g IV/IO  Diluted up to 10ml with normal saline
Using a 10ml syringe, dilute the medication with the required volume of NaCl to give a total volume of 7mls.

##### Type
Electrolyte solution [no schedule]

- **High-risk medication**

##### Presentations
2.5g in 5 ml (50% solution) – glass ampoule

##### Actions
Magnesium is the second most abundant intracellular cation. Less than 1% is present in extracellular fluid. Magnesium is involved in the processes regulating sodium and potassium movement across cell membranes and, as such, it may promote myocardial cell membrane stability.

#### Uses

- Torsades de pointes / polymorphic VT **- ICP only**

- Refractory VF (3rd drug) **- ICP only**

- Any seizure activity, or acute history of seizure activity, in patients with >20/40 gestation

- Symptomatic pre-eclampsia – hypertensive pregnant patient (20/40 to 6/52 post partum) presenting as unwell (altered LOC, headache, abdominal pain, visual disturbances, etc.) **- ICP only**

#### Adverse Effects
Rare – more common if serum magnesium is normal:

- Respiratory depression

- Nausea and vomiting

- Hypotension

- Confusion

- Bradycardia

#### Contraindications

- AV block

- Renal failure

- Hepatic failure

#### Precautions
Myasthenia gravis

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Safe to use

##### Breastfeeding
- Safe to use

#### Doses##### Adult
- **ICP: ****With cardiac output: **2.5g IV/IO 
- Diluted up to 10ml with normal saline
- Given over 5 minutes. 

- **ICP: ****Refractory VF: **2.5g IV/IO 
- Over 30-60 seconds 
- **ICP: ****Torsades de pointes: **2.5g IV/IO 
- Over 30-60 seconds 
- **ICP: ****Pre-eclampsia: **2.5g IV/IO via Springfusor 
- Made up to 7ml total volume with normal saline (7ml will run over 10 minutes).
- **Seizures due to eclampsia: **
- 2.5g IV/IO over 30-60 seconds
- Followed by 2.5g IV/IO via Springfusor - Made up to 7ml total volume with normal saline (7ml will run over 10 minutes).##### Paediatric
- Dose is 50mg/kg (to max of 2.5g)
- Dilute entire ampoule up to 10ml = 250mg/ml. Discard excess.
- **With cardiac output: **Give calculated dose IV/IO over 3 – 5 minutes.
- **No cardiac output: **Give calculated dose IV/IO over 30 – 60 seconds.
#### Special Notes
- Prolonged hypotension post-magnesium administration – if unresponsive to fluids, patient may be treated with IV calcium.

Adenosine.text: Record a 12 lead ECG prior to use of adenosine.
If patient has previously had an unpleasant (fearful) experience with adenosine, consider a pre-dose of midazolam 1 – 1.5mg IV.

##### Type
Endogenous purine nucleoside, found in all body cells [S4]

##### Presentations
6mg in 2ml – vial

##### Actions
- Causes transient inhibition of conduction in the heart, especially in the AV node.
- Onset: 5-10 seconds
- Duration: approximately 10 seconds

#### Uses

- Treatment of supraventricular tachycardia.

- Not for the treatment of atrial flutter or fibrillation; however, if mistakenly administered to patients in these arrhythmias, the decrease in AV conduction may unmask atrial activity. 

#### Adverse Effects
Common, although transient and generally minor:

- Arrhythmias at the time of conversion are common (up to 55% patients) – including PVCs, PACs, sinus bradycardia, AV blocks

- Transient flushing of the skin

- Mild dyspnoea

- Chest tightness

- Nausea

- Headache

- Feelings of apprehension and fear

#### Contraindications

- 2\xb0 or 3\xb0 heart block

- Known hypersensitivity

#### Precautions

- Asthma – may exacerbate bronchospasm

- Antagonised by: theophylline (Nuelin)

- Potentiated by: 
- dipyridamole (Persantin, Asasantin)
- carbamazepine (Tegretol, Teril)

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Safe to use

##### Breastfeeding
- Safe to use

#### Doses##### Adult (symptomatic adults only)
- **ICP: **6mg IV - rapid bolus (over 1 - 2 seconds) 
- Give into a fast flowing pump set.

- **ICP: **If first is unsuccessful: 
- Give second dose (2 minutes between doses)
- 12mg IV - rapid bolus (over 1 - 2 seconds).##### Paediatric
- **ICP:** First dose: 0.05mg/kg – given as above 
- **ICP:** Second dose: 0.1mg/kg
#### Special Notes
- Use only after Valsalva manoeuvre x 2.
- Record a 12 lead ECG prior to use of adenosine.
- Rapid injection with a pump set increases likelihood of success.
- Elevate limb if possible.
- Even if the arrhythmia is successfully converted, the patient should still be transported to hospital as the incidence of recurrent arrhythmias is quite high (10 – 15%).
- If patient has previously had an unpleasant (fearful) experience with adenosine, consider a pre-dose of midazolam 1 – 1.5mg IV.

Lignocaine.text: May be administered IM: Reconstitute each 1g vial with 3.5ml lignocaine. Draw up dose into two syringes and administer into two separate injection sites.

40mg through primed IO extension tubing, slowly over 2 minutes  Allow lignocaine to dwell in IO space for 60 seconds, then flush with 5-10ml of normal saline Repeat after 5 minutes if needed: 20mg over 60 seconds (max. 2 x repeats) Total max. dose 80mg
0.5mg/kg (to max. 40mg) through primed IO extension tubing, slowly over 2 minutes  Allow lignocaine to dwell in IO space for 60 seconds, then flush with 5-10ml of normal saline

In the conscious/semi conscious patient, the port line should be primed with the approved designated dose of Lignocaine. This is to be flushed with 1.2-1.5mls of normal saline in order to push the Lignocaine into the medullary space. Wait 2 to 3mins for Lignocaine to take effect.

##### Type

- Local anaesthetic

- Anti-arrhythmic [S4]

- **High-risk medication**

##### Presentations
50mg in 5ml – plastic ampoule

##### Actions

- Local anaesthetic effects

- Suppresses ventricular arrhythmias

- Onset SCI: 1 – 4 mins. Max effect: 5 – 10 minutes.

- Onset IV: 1 – 3 minutes.

#### Uses

- Local anaesthesia prior to invasive procedures

- VT with cardiac output, in patients who cannot have amiodarone

- Flushing of a painful IO in an aware patient

- Solvent for IM ceftriaxone administration

#### Adverse Effects
These effects are extremely unlikely in usual subcutaneous doses, especially if the syringe is continually aspirated. More likely if given IV.

- CNS effects: 
 stimulation followed by depression, drowsiness, agitation, muscle twitching, seizures, coma 

- Cardiac effects: 
 hypotension, bradycardia, heart block, asystole 

#### Contraindications
Known hypersensitivity 

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Safe to use

##### Breastfeeding
- Safe to use

#### Doses#### Local anaesthesia##### Adult and Paediatric
- **ICP:** Up to 5ml subcutaneously 

#### VT with cardiac output##### Adult and Paediatric
- **ICP:** 1mg/kg – slow IV / IO over 2 minutes 

#### IO in an aware patient##### Adult
- **ICP:** 40mg through primed IO extension tubing, slowly over 2 minutes 
- Allow lignocaine to dwell in IO space for 60 seconds, then flush with 5-10ml of normal saline
- Repeat after 5 minutes if needed: 20mg over 60 seconds (max. 2 x repeats)
- Total max. dose 80mg
- Consider systemic pain relief for patients not responding to IO lignocaine##### Paediatric
- **ICP:** 0.5mg/kg (to max. 40mg) through primed IO extension tubing, slowly over 2 minutes 
- Allow lignocaine to dwell in IO space for 60 seconds, then flush with 5-10ml of normal saline
- Repeat after 5 minutes if needed: half of initial dose over 60 seconds (max. 2 x repeats)
- Consider systemic pain relief for patients not responding to IO lignocaine
#### Solvent for IM Ceftriaxone
- AP and ICP refer to the medicine calculator for ceftriaxone for dose
